summary: Cross-chapter audit of cycle 01. REDUNDANCY: Manuscript-wide em-dash density is 36.6% of prose lines (876/2396), far exceeding the 10% threshold — every chapter exceeds 15%. 'He' sentence openers reach 9.25% (610/6592 sentences), exceeding 7%. The hedge word 'something' appears 343 times in 99,264 prose words (1 per 289), exceeding 1/800. Character re-introductions are pervasive: Doug's weight loss is re-described 8+ times, Tommy's physical package ('six-two, still built, crew cut, big hands, scar') is near-verbatim repeated 4 times, Cliff's chinos/polo/silver hair is restated in 5 chapters, and Paul's insurance-adjuster role is tagged 20+ times. Setting re-descriptions are significant: the great room (draft board + Lagavulin smell) is re-inventoried in 6+ chapters, the third step is mentioned 23 times with its explanation repeated, and the study is fully re-inventoried in Ch 11 after Ch 8. Loon calls appear 44 times and refrigerator cycling 35 times — both far exceed the 10-mention image threshold. CONSISTENCY: Four contradictory descriptions of the painting over the safe (Chs 2, 8, 11, 16). Joel's public defender tenure is given as 7, 12, and 20 years in three different chapters. The fire document's opening sentence changes between Ch 8 and Ch 16. The real estate deal is called 'the Burnsville thing' in Ch 1 but identified as being in Roseville in Ch 8. Tommy's school is named 'Eastview Lightning' in Ch 3 but located in 'Burnsville' in Ch 18.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 27: 'His face was thinner, the hoodie hung wrong on him, and the bags under his eyes looked permanent'; Ch 7 line 1731: 'Six feet of gaunt. Bags under his eyes that hadn't been there in June. The hoodie hanging off his shoulders like it was still on the hanger'
redundancy_findings.problem: Doug's gauntness/bags under eyes/hoodie hanging wrong are established in Ch 1 (line 27). Ch 7 restates all three traits in near-synonymous phrasing. This is the first of 8 separate descriptions of Doug's weight loss across the novel.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or compress the physical re-description at line 1731. Keep 'twenty-seven pounds since the divorce' as new information; cut the re-inventory of gaunt/bags/hoodie that the reader already has from Ch 1.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 7's opening does not re-describe Doug's gauntness, bags under eyes, or ill-fitting hoodie in terms synonymous with Chapter 1.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 7 line 1731: 'Six feet of gaunt...The hoodie hanging off his shoulders'; Ch 8 line 1938: 'Doug's weight loss on Thursday, the way the hoodies hung on him now, the jawline that used to be soft and was now sharp'
redundancy_findings.problem: Adjacent-chapter redundancy. Ch 7 just described Doug's weight loss and ill-fitting hoodie. Ch 8, the very next chapter, re-describes both from Cliff's POV.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the weight-loss/hoodie re-description at line 1938. Cliff can note Doug's withdrawal behavior rather than restating physical traits the reader just learned.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 8 does not re-describe Doug's weight loss or ill-fitting hoodie.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 27: 'His face was thinner, the hoodie hung wrong on him, and the bags under his eyes looked permanent'; Ch 13 line 3333: 'His hoodie was a size too big now. His face was thinner and the circles under his eyes had the permanence of something that wasn't going away'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 13 essentially rewrites Ch 1's introduction of Doug using near-synonymous phrasing for every detail. This is the 6th time the reader has been told Doug has lost weight.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the physical re-inventory at line 3333. The behavioral details (three bites counted, water-as-beer camouflage) are already strong and new. Remove the weight loss/hoodie/circles.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13's dinner scene does not re-describe Doug's weight loss, oversized hoodie, or under-eye circles.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 3333: 'Doug had lost weight...His face was thinner'; Ch 14 line 3520: 'Doug's weight was down twenty pounds from last September. His face was gaunt'
redundancy_findings.problem: Adjacent-chapter redundancy. Ch 13 just described Doug's weight loss and thinner face. Ch 14 restates both one chapter later. This is the 7th time.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the weight/gaunt restatement at line 3520. Keep the emotional content (Martin noticing, the remembered dialogue). Remove the physical re-inventory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 14 does not restate Doug's weight loss or facial gauntness in the dock-walk passage.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 45: 'pressed chinos and a collared polo...silver hair and a permanent tan'; Ch 12 line 2868: 'pressed chinos and his collared polo...His silver hair was combed'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 12 restates all three of Cliff's identifying traits from Ch 1 — pressed chinos, collared polo, silver hair — in a single sentence. The 'dressed like he was showing a property' observation was already made in Ch 7 (line 1787).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or reduce the physical re-inventory at line 2868 to at most one identifying detail. Enter through Cliff's behavior or body language.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 12 does not restate more than one of: pressed chinos, collared polo, silver hair.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'six-two and still built like something functional, crew cut graying at the temples'; Ch 14 line 3604: 'six-two and still built like the athlete he'd been, the crew cut and the big hands'; Ch 18 line 4662: 'Six-two, still built like the athlete he'd been, crew cut going gray at the temples...A scar on his left forearm'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 18 provides a near-verbatim reprise of Tommy's physical description from Ch 1 and Ch 14. The Mallick-summary format treats the reader as if they haven't met Tommy. This is the 4th time the 'six-two and still built' formula appears.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Radically compress Mallick's summary of Tommy at line 4662. Replace the physical re-inventory with Mallick's analytical insight — what she now understands about Tommy, not what he looks like.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 18's Mallick summary does not restate Tommy's height, build, crew cut, or scar in descriptive prose.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'six-two and still built like something functional, crew cut graying at the temples, hands that were always gripping something'; Ch 14 line 3604: 'six-two and still built like the athlete he'd been, the crew cut and the big hands and the scar on his forearm'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 14 re-packages Tommy's full physical introduction from Ch 1. 'Six-two and still built like the athlete he'd been' is a near-synonym of 'six-two and still built like something functional.' The scene is well-served by 'just the shape of him visible against the tree line' — cut the itemization.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 3604, cut the height/build/crew-cut/hands/scar list. Let the silhouette ('just the shape of him') carry the moment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 14's dock-scene description of Tommy does not restate his height, build, or crew cut.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'crew cut graying at the temples, hands that were always gripping something'; Ch 8 line 1942: 'big hands, the knuckles white from gripping. His crew cut was growing out unevenly, the gray at the temples catching the morning light'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 8 re-describes Tommy's crew cut graying at the temples and big gripping hands from Ch 1. Both details have appeared in at least two prior chapters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1942, cut the crew-cut and graying-temples re-description. Keep 'knuckles white from gripping' as a behavioral detail.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 8 does not re-describe Tommy's crew cut or graying temples.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 1944: 'Paul being the one she treated as the group's designated adult'; Ch 9 line 2345: 'Paul Decker, claims adjuster, mediator, the group's designated adult'
redundancy_findings.problem: Adjacent-chapter redundancy. 'The group's designated adult' appears verbatim in Ch 8 and Ch 9. Ch 9 also re-tags Paul as 'claims adjuster' — a role the reader has been told in at least 6 prior passages.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 2345, cut 'the group's designated adult' and reduce the appositional role-tag. Paul's name alone is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9 does not use the phrase 'designated adult.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 912: 'five-six, stocky, gray-streaked brown hair pulled back in a low bun, reading glasses on a beaded chain'; Ch 7 line 1819: 'five-six, stocky, the gray-streaked bun slightly loose on one side'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 7 re-describes Mallick's height (five-six), build (stocky), and hair (gray-streaked bun) from her Ch 4 introduction.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1819, cut height and build restatement. Doug can note something new about Mallick — the pen she clicks, her demeanor — without restating basic physical stats.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 7 does not restate Mallick's height or build.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 868: 'The draft board was still on the wall, Martin's picks circled in red Sharpie...smelled like Martin's scotch'; Ch 15 line 3713: 'The stone fireplace, the vaulted ceiling, the draft board still hanging on the far wall with Martin Kessler's picks circled in red marker. The smell of the house was the same — pine panels, old smoke, the ghost of Lagavulin'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 15 provides a full re-inventory of the great room — stone fireplace, vaulted ceiling, draft board with picks circled in red, Lagavulin smell. All of these have been described in multiple earlier chapters. The text says 'the smell of the house was the same,' signaling re-inventory.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 3713, lead with what has changed since Mallick's Saturday visit. Cut the fireplace/ceiling/draft-board/scotch-smell re-inventory. The chapter already notes the room's different energy — lead with that.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15's great-room entry does not re-list fireplace, ceiling, draft board, or Lagavulin smell as inventory items.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 868: 'The draft board was still on the wall, Martin's picks circled in red Sharpie'; Ch 13 lines 3141-3145: 'The draft board was still on the wall...Martin's picks were circled in red Sharpie...the great room still smelled faintly of the Lagavulin'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 13 re-inventories draft board (still on wall, picks circled in red) and Lagavulin smell — both established in Ch 4 and repeated in Chs 5, 7, 9. This is the 5th post-death mention of the draft board formula and 4th of the Lagavulin smell.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At lines 3141-3145, let the closing sentence ('if Nate squinted at the room from the right angle it almost looked like any other year') carry the work. Cut the re-inventory preamble.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13's great-room passage does not restate both the draft board on the wall and the Lagavulin smell.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 3141: 'The draft board was still on the wall'; Ch 14 line 3454: 'The draft board hung on the wall above the fireplace. Martin's picks were still circled in red marker'
redundancy_findings.problem: Adjacent-chapter redundancy. Ch 13 just told the reader the draft board was on the wall with picks circled in red. Ch 14 restates both one chapter later.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 3454, cut the draft-board restatement. If the board must appear, use a single glancing reference.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 14 does not restate that the draft board is on the wall or that picks are circled in red.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 8 lines 1950-1951: 'desk, filing cabinet, bookshelves...Tom Clancy novels...wall safe behind a framed print...combination dial one tick off'; Ch 11 lines 2727-2731: 'Martin's desk...bookshelves held financial manuals, thriller novels...The painting was crooked...the wall safe'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 11 fully re-inventories the study (desk, bookshelves, thriller novels, painting, safe) 3 chapters after Ch 8's thorough description. The reader has been in this room and knows its contents.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 2727, cut the re-inventory of bookshelves, novels, and desk. Paul can note what has changed (painting crooked, someone's been in the safe) without re-describing what hasn't.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 11 does not re-describe the study's desk, bookshelves, or their contents.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Paul's role tagged in Chs 2 (lines 478, 532), 3 (687), 5 (1253, 1269), 7 (1741), 8 (1934), 9 (2345, 2421); Ch 11 line 2703: 'He'd been an insurance claims adjuster for twenty-two years. He knew the paperwork of death'
redundancy_findings.problem: Paul's insurance-adjuster identity is re-proved as appositional role-tags at least 20 times across the novel. Ch 11 opens with a 3-sentence re-establishment after the reader has been told Paul is an insurance adjuster in every prior chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the explicit role re-establishment at line 2703. Let Paul's behavior demonstrate his profession without the narrator restating the label.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 11 does not use 'insurance claims adjuster' or 'claims adjuster' in its opening paragraphs.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 16 lines 4052, 4092, 4120: three insurance-role tags in one chapter — 'measured everything else...to the decimal,' 'Paul was an insurance man,' 'the insurance man's brain mapping the inputs to outputs'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 16 re-tags Paul as an insurance man three times within a single chapter, on top of 15+ prior role-tags across the novel.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to one insurance-role reference in Ch 16. Keep 'the insurance man's brain mapping the inputs to outputs' (line 4120); cut the other two.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 16 contains no more than one explicit insurance-role reference for Paul.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 18 lines 4654, 4842: 'the insurance adjuster's precision' and 'the insurance adjuster's measured delivery'
redundancy_findings.problem: The final chapter tags Paul as 'the insurance adjuster' twice more. Across the full novel, Paul has been identified by this role at least 20 times.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut both insurance-adjuster role-tags in Ch 18. Paul's behavior already demonstrates his professional habits.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 18 does not use 'insurance adjuster' as an appositional identifier for Paul.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Tommy's left forearm scar is physically described 13 times across the novel (Chs 2, 7, 12, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17, 18, 18, 18). Ch 13 line 3415: 'the scar that ran along the outside of it — pale, old, the kind of scar you stopped asking about.' Ch 13 has 4 scar mentions total.
redundancy_findings.problem: Tommy's scar exceeds 10 mentions. Target: keep 4-6 of the strongest (Ch 2 first sight, Ch 12 backstory, Ch 15 Mallick's forensic observation, Ch 18 confession). Ch 13's 4 scar mentions add no new information — the reader already knows it's pale and old.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 13, cut 2-3 of the 4 scar mentions. Retain at most one brief reference. The scar's significance is already established.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13 contains at most 1 reference to Tommy's forearm scar.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Tommy's scar appears 4 times in Ch 18 alone: line 4662 ('scar on his left forearm from a fire'), line 4700 ('old burn scar, a raised pale line'), line 4798 ('tremor...forearm...scar'), line 4810 ('old scar visible below the pushed-up sleeve'). The scar has been described 9 times before this chapter.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 18 re-describes the scar's physical appearance 4 times in a single chapter, after 9 prior descriptions across the novel. Target: keep at most 2 in Ch 18.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 18, cut 2 of the 4 scar mentions. Retain the confession-context reference (line 4662) and at most one during the arrest scene. Cut the re-descriptions at lines 4700 and 4810.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 18 contains at most 2 references to Tommy's forearm scar.

redundancy_findings.evidence: The word 'loon' appears 44 times across 18 chapters (all chapters, 1-4 per chapter). Ch 1 has 4 loon mentions. Not tracked as a motif or setup/payoff in outline/outline.md.
redundancy_findings.problem: Loon calls at 44 mentions far exceed the 10-mention threshold. Target: keep 8-10 of the strongest instances (e.g., Ch 1 first establishment, Ch 3 'idiot laugh,' Ch 14 discovery, Ch 18 final). Ch 1 has 4; keep 1-2.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Across the manuscript, reduce loon mentions from 44 to 8-10. In Ch 1, cut 2-3 of the 4 mentions, retaining one atmospheric establishment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 1 contains at most 2 loon references.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Loon mentions: Ch 2 (4), Ch 11 (4), Ch 12 (3), Ch 14 (3), Ch 17 (3). Total: 44 across 18 chapters. Target: keep 8-10.
redundancy_findings.problem: Chs 2, 11, 12, 14, and 17 each have 3-4 loon mentions. At 44 total, the image has become wallpaper rather than atmosphere. Cut 2-3 per chapter from these high-count chapters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In each of Chs 2, 11, 12, 14, and 17, cut at least 2 loon mentions, retaining the single strongest atmospheric use per chapter.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Each of Chs 2, 11, 12, 14, and 17 contains at most 1 loon reference.

redundancy_findings.evidence: The third-step-that-creaks is mentioned 23+ times across 14 chapters. Chs 1 and 2 have 4 mentions each, Ch 4 has 3. The explanatory formula 'everyone knew to skip the third step' is restated in Chs 1, 2, 4, 7, 11, 18.
redundancy_findings.problem: At 23 mentions, the third step far exceeds 10. The explanatory formula ('everyone knew to skip it') is re-taught 6 times. Target: keep 8-10 total, with the explanation appearing at most twice (Ch 1 establishment, one later callback). Ch 4 (3 mentions, including line 1122 re-explaining 'twenty-five years without ever fixing it') is the most redundant chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 4, cut 2 of the 3 third-step mentions. Retain one functional reference (the creak as a sound cue). Cut the re-explanation at line 1122.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 4 contains at most 1 third-step reference and does not re-explain that everyone knows to skip it.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Refrigerator cycling/humming appears 35 times across the novel. The '40-minute cycle' detail appears in Chs 2, 4, 5, 10, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18. Ch 10 has the highest concentration (8 mentions of 'refrigerator' including the 'forty-minute' establishment and 'glasses rattling in the cabinet').
redundancy_findings.problem: At 35 mentions, refrigerator cycling exceeds 10. The '40-minute cycle' is stated in 9 chapters. Target: keep 8-10 total, including the Ch 10 establishment and Ch 18 closing reference. Ch 10 has 8 refrigerator mentions — keep 2-3, cut the rest.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 10, reduce refrigerator mentions from 8 to 2-3. Retain the strongest establishment of the timing motif; cut the repetitive cycling/humming references.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 10 contains at most 3 refrigerator references.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 7 line 1885: 'walked down the stone path through the pines and the birch, eighty yards'; Ch 10 line 2678: 'stone path that led through the pines and birches to the dock — eighty yards'; Ch 15 line 3751: 'stone path from the deck to the dock was eighty yards through a corridor of white pine and paper birch'; Ch 17 line 4487: 'Eighty yards away, down the stone path through the pines and birch, the dock extended thirty feet'
redundancy_findings.problem: The formulation 'stone path...eighty yards...pines and birch...dock' appears near-verbatim in 4 chapters (7, 10, 15, 17). Ch 15 is most redundant since the reader has traversed this path twice before.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 15 (line 3751), cut the stone-path/eighty-yards/pines-and-birch geographic template. Mallick can arrive at the dock without re-teaching the reader the distance and terrain.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15 does not restate the eighty-yard distance or the stone-path-through-pines-and-birch formula.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 10 line 2678: 'the dock that extended thirty feet into the lake'; Ch 17 line 4487: 'Eighty yards away, down the stone path through the pines and birch, the dock extended thirty feet into the black water'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 17 restates both the eighty-yard distance and the thirty-foot dock length, near-verbatim from Ch 10. Both measurements have been given before.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 17, cut the eighty-yards and thirty-feet measurements. The dock can appear without restating its dimensions.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 17 does not restate the dock's thirty-foot length or the eighty-yard path distance.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 560: 'Joel had taken the chair by the dead fireplace and was already asleep, or pretending to be — his legs stretched out, his chin on his chest, his breathing even. Joel's ability to shut down under pressure was either a lawyer's skill or a dissociative gift'; Ch 2 line 632: 'Joel was still in the chair by the fireplace, chin on chest, eyes closed. His breathing was too regular for real sleep — Joel was playing a role, or Joel had genuinely found a way to turn off'
redundancy_findings.problem: Within Ch 2, Joel's chin-on-chest/fireplace-chair/sleeping-or-performing pose is described near-identically 72 lines apart. Same physical position, same observation about whether Joel is genuinely sleeping.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 632, cut the re-description of Joel's posture and the sleeping-or-performing observation. A brief 'Joel hadn't moved' is sufficient since the reader just received this image.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 2's second mention of Joel in the fireplace chair does not re-describe his posture or re-state the sleeping-or-performing observation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 45 em-dash-containing prose lines / 77 total prose lines = 58.4%. Manuscript-wide: 876/2396 = 36.6%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 12 has the highest em-dash density in the manuscript at 58.4% — nearly 3 of every 5 prose lines contain an em-dash. Manuscript-wide density is 36.6%, far above the 10% threshold. Target: below 8% manuscript-wide.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines in Ch 12 from 45 to ~6. Convert parenthetical asides to commas, colons, or new sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest 1-2 interruptions per page.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 12's em-dash density is below 10% of prose lines.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 70 em-dash-containing prose lines / 130 total prose lines = 53.8%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 6 em-dash density is 53.8%. Over half of all prose lines contain an em-dash.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash lines from 70 to ~10. Use commas, colons, periods, or subordinate clauses instead.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 6's em-dash density is below 10%.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 45 em-dash-containing prose lines / 87 total prose lines = 51.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 9 em-dash density is 51.7%.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash lines from 45 to ~7. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interruptions.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9's em-dash density is below 10%.

redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 15 em-dash density is 48.1%.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash lines from 51 to ~8.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15's em-dash density is below 10%.

redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary sentence openings in Ch 4. Use character names, prepositional phrases, temporal markers, action-first constructions, or subordinate clauses to reduce He-openers to below 7%.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 42 occurrences of 'something' / 6,963 prose words = 1 per 165 words. Manuscript-wide: 343/99,264 = 1 per 289 (threshold: 1 per 800).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 3 has the highest 'something' density at 1 per 165 words — nearly 5x the threshold. Tommy's POV uses 'something' as a hedging filler in nearly every paragraph.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace or cut at least 30 of the 42 'something' instances in Ch 3. In most cases, name the thing specifically or cut the hedge entirely. 'Something about X' should become 'X.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 3 contains no more than 8 instances of 'something.'

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 616: 'the landscape print on the wall — the one with the lake and the pines, the one Martin's parents had bought at a craft fair in Duluth in 1983'; Ch 8 line 1950: 'a framed print of a lake that looked nothing like Elbow Lake, a stock image in a frame that cost more than the print'; Ch 11 line 2731: 'an oil painting Martin's mother had commissioned from a local artist two decades ago'; Ch 16 line 4148: 'a lake scene that didn't look anything like Elbow Lake but that Martin had bought at a craft fair in Pelican Rapids because the frame was nice'
consistency_findings.problem: The painting over the safe is described with four contradictory origin stories: craft fair in Duluth bought by Martin's parents (Ch 2), stock image (Ch 8), oil painting commissioned by Martin's mother (Ch 11), craft fair in Pelican Rapids bought by Martin (Ch 16). These cannot all be correct. The painting is a recurring plot object central to the safe discovery sequence.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Establish one canonical description of the painting in Ch 2 (the first mention) and ensure Chs 8, 11, and 16 use consistent language. Decide: is it a print from a craft fair or a commissioned painting? Who bought it — Martin, his parents, or his mother? Where — Duluth or Pelican Rapids?
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: All four chapters describe the painting's origin consistently.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 2: 'Martin's parents had bought at a craft fair in Duluth in 1983'; Ch 11 line 2731: 'an oil painting Martin's mother had commissioned from a local artist two decades ago, competent but uninspired'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch 11 contradicts Ch 2 on the painting's nature (commissioned oil painting vs. craft fair print) and origin (Martin's mother commissioned vs. Martin's parents bought).
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Align Ch 11's painting description with the canonical version established in Ch 2. Replace the commissioned-painting detail with the correct origin.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 11 describes the painting consistently with Chapter 2.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 2: 'craft fair in Duluth in 1983'; Ch 16 line 4148: 'Martin had bought at a craft fair in Pelican Rapids because the frame was nice'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch 16 contradicts Ch 2 on the buyer (Martin vs. Martin's parents) and location (Pelican Rapids vs. Duluth).
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Align Ch 16 with the canonical painting description.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 16 describes the painting's origin consistently with Chapter 2.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 866: 'Seven years as a public defender in Hennepin County'; Ch 16 line 4068: 'watched this from the other side for twelve years'; Ch 16 line 4266: 'spent twelve years building that scaffolding'; Ch 17 line 4365: 'Twenty years as a public defender'
consistency_findings.problem: Joel's public defender tenure is stated as 7 years (Ch 4), 12 years (Ch 16), and 20 years (Ch 17). Joel is 47 years old. These three numbers cannot all be correct.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Pick one consistent tenure length for Joel. If Joel is 47 and started after law school (~age 25-27), 20 years is most plausible. Correct Chs 4 and 16 to match.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Joel's public defender tenure is stated as the same number in all chapters.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 866: 'Seven years as a public defender'; Ch 16 lines 4068, 4266: 'twelve years'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch 16 states Joel has been a public defender for 12 years, contradicting Ch 4's 7 years and Ch 17's 20 years.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Correct the tenure number in Ch 16 to match the canonical value.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 16's references to Joel's tenure match the canonical number.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 17 line 4365: 'Twenty years as a public defender'; contradicts Ch 4 (7 years) and Ch 16 (12 years).
consistency_findings.problem: Ch 17 states Joel has been a public defender for 20 years, contradicting the other two values.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Correct the tenure number in Ch 17 to match the canonical value.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 17's reference to Joel's tenure matches the canonical number.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 2010: '*I'm writing this down because somebody should, and because I don't trust myself to remember it right if I don't write it now while it's still clear. This is what happened at the cabin on Wolf Creek Road, Washburn County, Wisconsin*'; Ch 16 line 4190: '*I'm writing this because I need a record of what happened and I don't trust myself to remember it right if I wait. Tommy and I were at the Lindquist cabin in Washburn County, Wisconsin*'
consistency_findings.problem: The fire document is a fixed, handwritten text. Its opening sentence changes between Ch 8 and Ch 16: 'because somebody should' vs. 'because I need a record'; 'the cabin on Wolf Creek Road' vs. 'the Lindquist cabin'; 'write it now while it's still clear' vs. 'remember it right if I wait.' A handwritten document cannot have two different openings.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Use the same verbatim text for the fire document in both Ch 8 and Ch 16. Pick one version of the opening and standardize the cabin name (Wolf Creek Road or Lindquist cabin, not both).
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The fire document's quoted text is identical in both chapters.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 253: 'close out our position on the Burnsville thing for two years'; Ch 8 line 1928: 'a duplex in Roseville that had gone sideways'; Ch 8 line 1980: 'the Roseville property at 1847 Dale St'
consistency_findings.problem: The real estate deal between Martin and Cliff is called 'the Burnsville thing' in Ch 1 but identified as being in Roseville in Ch 8. Burnsville and Roseville are different suburbs of Minneapolis, 20+ miles apart.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Correct Ch 1's 'the Burnsville thing' to 'the Roseville thing' or adjust Ch 8's location to match. Roseville is established more specifically (1847 Dale St), so correcting Ch 1 is simpler.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The real estate deal location is named consistently across Chs 1 and 8.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 3 line 797: 'head coach of the Eastview Lightning'; Ch 18 line 4662: 'High school football coach and PE teacher, Burnsville, suburban Minneapolis'
consistency_findings.problem: Tommy's school is named Eastview Lightning in Ch 3 but his location is given as Burnsville in Ch 18. In reality, Eastview High School is in Apple Valley, not Burnsville. Either the school name or the suburb needs to be corrected for internal consistency.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Align Tommy's school name and location. Either change 'Burnsville' in Ch 18 to the suburb matching the Eastview school, or change the school name in Ch 3.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Tommy's school name and location are consistent across all chapters.
